text:
Here's a complete REST API design for your expense tracker.

**Phase 1 assumptions:** Single user per account (personal app), JWT auth, mobile/web frontend consumers, low traffic.

---

## Resource Map

```
POST   /v1/auth/register
POST   /v1/auth/login
POST   /v1/auth/refresh
POST   /v1/auth/logout

GET    /v1/users/me
PATCH  /v1/users/me

GET    /v1/categories
POST   /v1/categories
GET    /v1/categories/{id}
PATCH  /v1/categories/{id}
DELETE /v1/categories/{id}

GET    /v1/expenses
POST   /v1/expenses
GET    /v1/expenses/{id}
PATCH  /v1/expenses/{id}
DELETE /v1/expenses/{id}

GET    /v1/summaries/monthly?year=2026&month=6
GET    /v1/summaries/yearly?year=2026
```

---

Now writing the OpenAPI spec:

text:
cal_date` (date string) on each expense at write time representing the user's local date. Use that field for filtering and summary bucketing, not UTC timestamp.

**7. Percentage math ownership (Arjuna P0 partial + Duryodhana #3 + Vidura test #1)**
Independently-rounded per-category percentages will sum to 99% or 101%. Fix: return raw `total` per category plus the grand total in summary responses; document that percentage is derived. If you compute server-side, specify largest-remainder rounding and guarantee the invariant.

**8. Pagination metadata missing (Arjuna P1 + Duryodhana #7)**
`GET /expenses` has no `next_cursor`/`has_more` in the described response. The cursor must also encode a composite `(sort_key, id)` to handle ties and prevent rows being skipped/duplicated when data mutates mid-pagination.

**9. Rate limiting and brute force unspecified (Bhishma P1)**
`/auth/login` and `/auth/refresh` have no throttling. An 8-character password floor is brute-forceable without it. Define: per-IP + per-account limits, exponential backoff after N failures on login.

**10. No idempotency on expense creation (Arjuna P1)**
A network retry on `POST /expenses` double-books the spend. Support `Idempotency-Key` header — the server deduplicates within a time window and returns the original response on replay.

---

### Design Improvements — Strongly Recommended

**11. Unpaginated category list + fat embedded category (Arjuna P2 + Duryodhana #5)**
A 100-expense page embeds the same category object 100 times. Rename propagates stale copies. Fix: embed only `{ id, name, color, icon }` in expense responses (not the full Category), and add pagination to `GET /categories` before users accumulate dozens.

**12. Response envelope inconsistency (Arjuna P1)**
Collections use `{ data: [] }`, single resources are bare, auth returns a flat shape. Pick one convention — everything wrapped in `data` or nothing — and apply it uniformly.

**13. Summary endpoints too rigid (Arjuna P2 + Duryodhana #8)**
Two hard-coded shapes won't survive the first "last 90 days" request. Generalize to `GET /summaries?group_by=month&from=&to=`. Also: yearly summary has no query caching — it's an unbounded live aggregate that degrades as the account ages. Materialize monthly rollups on expense write.

**14. Mass assignment on PATCH (Bhishma P2)**
All PATCH handlers must bind to an explicit allowlist DTO. Block client-supplied `id`, `user_id`, `created_at`, `is_system`.

---

### Test Cases You Must Have (Vidura's must-runs)

| # | Case | Why |
|---|------|-----|
| 1 | Monthly summary: totals + percentages sum correctly | Float rounding + largest-remainder |
| 2 | Summary for month with zero expenses | Divide-by-zero in `average_per_day` |
| 3 | Category deletion cascade | Expenses survive under Uncategorized |
| 4 | Date filter with `from=to` (single day) | Inclusive/exclusive boundary |
| 5 | Cursor exhaustion with no duplicate IDs | Off-by-one on last page |
| 6 | Old refresh token rejected after rotation | Token rotation works |
| 7 | Refresh after logout returns 401 | Logout is a real security boundary |
| 8 | User B cannot read User A's expense | IDOR regression test |

---

### Consensus Priority Order

| Rank | Issue | Flagged by |
|------|-------|------------|
| 1 | Store currency + minor units per Expense | Arjuna, Duryodhana |
| 2 | BOLA ownership check on all `/{id}` routes | Bhishma, Vidura |
| 3 | Soft-delete categories, preserve history | Arjuna, Duryodhana |
| 4 | Account enumeration fix | Bhishma |
| 5 | Refresh token rotation + reuse detection | Bhishma, Duryodhana, Vidura |
| 6 | Store `local_date` per expense for TZ-safe filtering | Duryodhana |
| 7 | Idempotency-Key on POST /expenses | Arjuna |
| 8 | Rate limiting on auth endpoints | Bhishma |

Items 1–3 affect the correctness of your core data model and should be resolved before any schema or code is written. Items 4–8 are implementation constraints that belong in the initial build, not a follow-up patch.
